title: Balatonboglár

infobox.key: [MP](/wiki/List_of_members_of_the_National_Assembly_of_Hungary_(2018%E2%80%9322) "List of members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2018–22)")

infobox.value: * Balatonboglár * [Balatonlelle](/wiki/Balatonlelle "Balatonlelle") * [Fonyód](/wiki/Fony%C3%B3d "Fonyód") (district seat) * [Lengyeltóti](/wiki/Lengyelt%C3%B3ti "Lengyeltóti") | [Coat of arms - Fonyód](/wiki/File:HUN_Fony%C3%B3d_C%C3%ADmer.svg "Coat of arms - Fonyód")

is a [resort town](/wiki/Resort_town "Resort town") situated on the south shore of [Lake Balaton](/wiki/Lake_Balaton "Lake Balaton") in [Somogy County](/wiki/Somogy_County "Somogy County"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"). It is the official centre of the [Balatonboglár wine region](/wiki/Balatonbogl%C3%A1r_wine_region "Balatonboglár wine region"), and is often called the "town of grapes and wine."
["U.S. Department of State: Report on Global Anti-Semitism"](https://2001-2009.state.gov/g/drl/rls/40258.htm). 2005-01-05. Retrieved 2008-09-24.

Gyorgyi Jakobi (2004-09-04). ["Hero or traitor? - statue of a controversial Hungarian Prime Minister unveiled, but not in Budapest"](https://web.archive.org/web/20110718183205/http://incentraleurope.radio.cz/ice/article/52609). Archived from [the original](http://incentraleurope.radio.cz/ice/article/52609) on 2011-07-18. Retrieved 2009-09-24.

**Balatonboglár** is a [resort town](/wiki/Resort_town "Resort town") situated on the south shore of [Lake Balaton](/wiki/Lake_Balaton "Lake Balaton") in [Somogy County](/wiki/Somogy_County "Somogy County"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"). It is the official centre of the [Balatonboglár wine region](/wiki/Balatonbogl%C3%A1r_wine_region "Balatonboglár wine region"), and is often called the "town of grapes and wine."

Between 1979 and 1991 Balatonboglár formed a single settlement together with [Balatonlelle](/wiki/Balatonlelle "Balatonlelle") under the name [Boglárlelle](/wiki/Bogl%C3%A1rlelle "Boglárlelle").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balatonbogl%C3%A1r&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The region has been populated for more than six thousand years. The area was first documented in 1211. The first bathing association was established in 1904, and Balatonboglár was declared a medicinal bath in 1912. Balatonboglár and [Balatonlelle](/wiki/Balatonlelle "Balatonlelle") were unified as Boglárlelle from 1979 until October 1, 1991.

The Xantus look-out tower, the symbol of the city, resides on top of a hill and is well lit at night. It is named for [John Xantus de Vesey](/wiki/John_Xantus_de_Vesey "John Xantus de Vesey").

## Main sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balatonbogl%C3%A1r&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Main sights")]

### Statue of Pál Teleki

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Balatonbogl%C3%A1r&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Statue of Pál Teleki")]

The city is host to a statue honoring a controversial figure in Hungarian history, [Pál Teleki](/wiki/P%C3%A1l_Teleki "Pál Teleki"), who was twice prime minister of Hungary. Teleki was regarded by some as a flawed hero and he remained a controversial person long after his death. This was reflected in a public dispute covered in the Hungarian media during early 2004 over a statue of him, marking the 63rd anniversary of his death. The statue by sculptor [Tibor Rieger](/w/index.php?title=Tibor_Rieger&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tibor Rieger (page does not exist)") [[hu](https://hu.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rieger_Tibor "hu:Rieger Tibor")] was originally to be installed opposite the President's official residence in [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"). However, the Minister of Culture [István Hiller](/wiki/Istv%C3%A1n_Hiller "István Hiller"), following pressure from the [Wiesenthal Center](/wiki/Wiesenthal_Center "Wiesenthal Center"), canceled these plans. On 5 April 2004, the statue was finally placed in the courtyard of the Catholic Church in Balatonboglár.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Balatonboglar had during World War II been host to thousands of Polish refugees who opened in that town one of only two secondary schools for Poles in Europe during 1939-1944. They credited Teleki with opening Hungary's borders to them and named a street in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") for him after the war ended.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
